summary: My great-grandfather's old home was located right on the corner where Fayette County, Fulton County, and Coweta County met. The house still stands, though the county lines have shifted over time. Fayette County was also where Frank Jones was jailed, and where I had to deal with some complicated land and legal matters involving the Shropshire family.

Fulton County is where part of my family's old property ended up after Campbell County merged with it. I also had a large cattle farm there with over 200 acres and a lake, which I later sold. My early legal career was based in Fulton County, where I worked as Assistant District Attorney and Solicitor General under John Boykin.

Coweta County was one of the three counties converging near my great-grandfather's old home. The house was unique because it actually sat in parts of Fayette, Fulton, and Coweta counties. This area is deeply tied to my family's history and land ownership.

Campbell County used to be one of the counties where my family's old home was located before it was merged into Fulton County. The county lines changed, moving about 300 feet south, which affected where the house officially sat. This change is part of the local history I grew up with.

The Shropshire family, a black family who bought land in my area after the Civil War, might have originally come from what is now Henry County. I don't know exactly when or how, but that connection was mentioned when discussing their origins. Henry County is part of the broader regional history tied to my land and neighbors.

The Shropshire family were black landowners who came into this area after the Civil War and bought a couple hundred acres. Otis Shropshire was the patriarch who died intestate, and I had to get a quit claim deed from Mrs. Gray, a Shropshire descendant, to clear the title on some of the land. Their presence and land dealings were important in how I eventually reacquired property in Fayette County.

Frank Jones was a mean and illiterate man who bought land from the Shropshire family but failed to keep up with payments. He killed a man, allegedly over a bootlegging dispute, and was jailed in Fayette County. I was approached by his wife Lizzie and her uncle Wright Knox to represent him, though I warned them the case was tough given the racial prejudices at the time.

Lizzie Arnold Jones was Frank Jones’s wife and came from the Arnold black family who owned their own land nearby. She was smart and came with her uncle Wright Knox to ask me to represent Frank when he was jailed for murder. Lizzie was involved in trying to save their land from foreclosure, which I eventually helped with by paying off the debt.

Wright Knox was Lizzie Arnold Jones’s uncle who came with her to see me about representing Frank Jones. He was part of the effort to save their land from foreclosure and was living in the household of Otis Shropshire before he died. Wright Knox played a key role in connecting me to the Jones family’s legal troubles.

I was actually born near Palmetto, Georgia, about four or five miles from the old family property. Palmetto is part of the local area where my family roots run deep. Growing up there gave me a strong connection to the land and the surrounding counties.
cues: four or five miles from family property

summary: I graduated from Oglethorpe University with second honors, which was a proud achievement for me. This was an important step before I went on to study law. Oglethorpe helped lay the foundation for my later legal career.

I attended Emory University School of Law and earned my Juris Doctorate degree there. Interestingly, I passed the Georgia Bar before I even finished law school. Emory was where I prepared for my legal career and started practicing law.

I took the Georgia Bar exam early, even before finishing law school, and passed it. Passing the Bar was a significant milestone that allowed me to resign my auditor job and begin practicing law. It was a challenge I took on with confidence, expecting many to fail, but I succeeded.

After passing the Bar, I worked as an Assistant District Attorney in Fulton County. At the time, the position was also called Solicitor General. I was the youngest man in the office and gained valuable experience before moving on to other legal work.

The role of Solicitor General in Fulton County was essentially the same as Assistant District Attorney when I served. I worked under John Boykin and handled prosecutions during a politically charged era. This position gave me insight into the justice system and local politics.

John Boykin was the head of the office where I worked as Assistant District Attorney or Solicitor General in Fulton County. I was the youngest man in his office and learned a lot from that experience. He was a significant figure in my early legal career.

During my time practicing law, I was involved in the 1938 political campaign when Franklin D. Roosevelt tried to defeat Senator Walter F. George. Roosevelt supported Lawrence Camp in that race. It was a notable moment in Georgia politics that I witnessed firsthand.
cues: tried to defeat Walter F. George

summary: Walter F. George was the incumbent U.S. Senator from Georgia during the 1938 election. Roosevelt tried to unseat him by backing Lawrence Camp. George was a key figure in Georgia politics and the election was closely contested.

Lawrence Camp was a good friend of mine and the candidate Roosevelt picked to run against Walter F. George in 1938. I used to caddy for him on the golf course. That election was a tough three-way race and Camp was at the center of it.

Eugene Talmadge was another candidate in the 1938 Senate race and nearly won. The election was very close, and there were questions about the results. If Talmadge had won, he would have gone to the U.S. Senate instead of becoming governor later.

entity: United States Senate election in Georgia, 1938
summary: The 1938 U.S. Senate election in Georgia was a three-way primary involving Walter F. George, Lawrence Camp, and Eugene Talmadge. Roosevelt backed Camp in an attempt to unseat George. The county unit system played a big role, and the election was very close with some controversy over the results.

The county unit system was used in Georgia elections at the time and was very undemocratic. For example, Fulton County with half a million people had only six votes, while small counties with far fewer people had more votes. This system influenced the 1938 Senate election and shaped political outcomes in the state.

Statesboro, Georgia was mentioned as one of the small counties with very few people but still having votes under the county unit system. It exemplified the imbalance in voting power that affected elections like the 1938 Senate race. This kind of place showed how the system was skewed against populous areas.
